content:
System Administration

DevOps

Cloud Computing

Windows

Programming

3 min read
Just now

--

I’m sharing this because it needs to be said out loud.
Eleven days.
That’s how long an attacker had valid credentials in our environment before we detected anything. Not because our security was catastrophically bad. Because most environments — including good ones — have detection gaps that aren’t obvious until something goes wrong.

How it started
A user clicked a phishing link. Their credentials were captured. Standard attack vector, happens thousands of times a day across thousands of organisations.
What happened next is where it gets instructive.
The attacker didn’t immediately do anything obvious. They didn’t encrypt files. They didn’t exfiltrate gigabytes of data. They moved quietly — logging in during business hours from an IP address that appeared to be in a plausible location, accessing only the systems the compromised user had access to, not triggering any volume-based alerts.
This is modern attacker behaviour. Slow, deliberate, patient.

What finally caught it
An engineer noticed something in a routine log review that didn’t match a pattern she’d seen before. The compromised user’s account had accessed a file share they hadn’t accessed in months. Not a lot of files. Just a few. But not consistent with that user’s normal behaviour.
She pulled the authentication logs. The login times were slightly off — the user always logged in between 9am and 6pm. One session had started at 7:43am from a slightly different client machine name.
Slight. Not obvious. Enough.

The detection scripts we implemented after
# Detect logins outside normal business hours
Get-WinEvent -ComputerName "DC01" -FilterHashtable @{
LogName = 'Security'
Id = 4624
StartTime = (Get-Date).AddDays(-1)
} |
Where-Object {
$hour = $_.TimeCreated.Hour
($hour -lt 7 -or $hour -gt 20) -and
$_.Properties[8].Value -eq 10
} |
Select-Object TimeCreated,
@{n='User';e={$_.Properties[5].Value}},
@{n='SourceIP';e={$_.Properties[18].Value}} |
Export-Csv "C:\Reports\AfterHoursLogins.csv" -NoTypeInformation# Detect new machines accessing domain resources
# Compare this week's unique workstation names vs last week's
$thisWeek = Get-WinEvent -ComputerName "DC01" -FilterHashtable @{
LogName='Security'; Id=4624
StartTime=(Get-Date).AddDays(-7)
} | ForEach-Object {$_.Properties[11].Value} | Sort-Object -Unique
$lastWeek = Get-WinEvent -ComputerName "DC01" -FilterHashtable @{
LogName='Security'; Id=4624
StartTime=(Get-Date).AddDays(-14)
EndTime=(Get-Date).AddDays(-7)
} | ForEach-Object {$_.Properties[11].Value} | Sort-Object -Unique
Compare-Object $lastWeek $thisWeek | Where-Object {$_.SideIndicator -eq "=>"}New machines appearing that weren’t there last week are worth investigating.

The three gaps this incident exposed
We had alerting on volume. We didn’t have alerting on behaviour. A user accessing 5 files on an unusual share doesn’t trigger a volume alert. It’s anomalous but not loud.
We had logging enabled. We weren’t reviewing it consistently. Logs that nobody reads are the same as no logs.
We had MFA on external access. We didn’t have MFA on internal lateral movement. Once inside the network, the attacker moved freely.
All three are fixable. None of them are exotic security requirements. They’re table stakes that had fallen off the priority list.

Eleven days is a long time. It’s also shorter than the industry average dwell time, which is measured in weeks to months.
The attacker was in your environment before the alert fired. Your job is to shrink the time between entry and detection.
Start with the after-hours login script. Run it tomorrow morning. See what it finds.
— Prasanta
